games.explanation: Gausman has been the same pitcher he always is, which is to say a fastball-splitter guy who lives in the zone and gets touched up when right-handed bats stop chasing. Tampa happens to roll out exactly that kind of lineup, with Aranda, Díaz, and Caminero all sporting xwOBAs north of .340 against righties and a collective allergy to swinging at junk. Rasmussen is quietly the better arm in this game, with a barrel rate against that makes the Toronto power profile look a lot less scary than the names suggest. The board is pricing this like a coin flip between equals, and our number says the Rays are closer to a 60% favorite. At -125, you're getting the better team, the better starter, and the better matchup, all at one price.

The market is treating this like Sánchez walks through a lineup he's never seen, and that ignores the fact that he's been distinctly mortal against right-handed bats all year. The Athletics roll out a righty-heavy order with real thump in Kurtz and Langeliers, and Sánchez's splits get a lot uglier when lefties aren't in the box (a .289 xwOBA against righties versus the dominant number everyone keeps quoting). Severino isn't winning any Cy Youngs, but a coin-flip game by Elo shouldn't be priced like a mismatch, and the wind blowing across a homer-friendly yard cuts both ways. At +159 on a team the simulation has within a run, you're getting paid like an underdog in a game that profiles as a toss-up.

Taj Bradley keeps getting priced like the steady veteran he isn't, and tonight that ends with a Washington lineup specifically built to expose him. Bradley has been a different pitcher against lefties all year, with a barrel rate north of 10% and a walk rate that turns at-bats into landmines, which is bad news with James Wood and Luis García Jr. waiting. The Twins counter with Buxton and a lot of strikeouts, against a Cavalli profile that misses bats at a 25% whiff clip and keeps the barrels off the board. A near pick'em on the better team, in a park that plays neutral, is the kind of price you take and stop asking questions.

deGrom's name still moves lines like it's 2018, and the market keeps pricing him as if the Yankees lineup is a problem he's solved before. It isn't. Judge has been a different species against right-handed pitching this year, Rice has quietly posted a .412 xwOBA off righties, and Bellinger gives this order a fourth bat that punishes anything middle-in. New York is also the better team on paper by a wide Elo margin, which makes -118 feel like a number built on reputation rather than the matchup in front of it.

Gavin Williams has the strikeout numbers of an ace and the walk numbers of a guy pitching himself out of the rotation, which is exactly the profile Kansas City's lineup is built to punish. Witt and Pasquantino don't chase, Garcia puts everything in play, and a starter walking nearly 12 percent of righties is going to hand them free baserunners all night. Kolek isn't a household name but he's been roughly Williams's equal in the run prevention that actually matters, and the Royals quietly grade out as the better team here by a meaningful margin. Getting plus money on the side our numbers make a slight favorite is the entire ballgame.

Ohtani the pitcher is being priced like Ohtani the myth, and the gap between those two things is where this ticket lives. The Astros run out a right-handed lineup against an arm that has been distinctly more mortal versus righties, where the xwOBA-against climbs into the .240s and the strikeout rate dips into the high 20s. Correa and Alvarez are the kind of bats that punish anyone working in the zone with diminished swing-and-miss, and a closed roof takes weather variance off the table in a park that already plays neutral. Houston only needs to hang around in a projected one-run game, and at +200 you are getting paid like they are an afterthought.

Kirby's name still carries ace pricing, but the underlying profile has slipped into the realm of mortal mid-rotation, and the market hasn't caught up. He's been getting barreled at a near-double-digit clip and giving up hard contact on nearly half the balls in play, which is exactly the wrong matchup for an Olson-Baldwin core that punishes righties. Elder isn't a star, but the roof and the park quietly do a lot of work for him against a Seattle lineup that lives and dies with Raleigh. Getting plus money on the better team, in the better environment, against a starter the data has been whispering about for months is the entire pitch at +120.

Ohtani's numbers against righties look pristine until you notice the lineup he's faced and the lineup he's facing tonight, which are not the same caliber of problem. Houston runs Correa, Alvarez, Paredes and Walker at him, a group that punishes mistakes from anyone, and the simulation has this landing as a one-run game rather than the blowout the price implies. The market sees Ohtani's sub-.250 xwOBA-against and stops reading; the runline cover sits closer to 58% once you weight for opponent quality. Plus money on a Houston team that only needs to lose by one is the entire pitch, and +120 is generous for a coin flip.

Kirby's name still carries ace pricing, but the underlying profile has slipped into the realm of mortal mid-rotation, and the market hasn't caught up.
